heb.paragraph: The verb exagorazomenoi (v. 5, ‘making the most of every opportunity’) literally means ‘buying up the time.’ The kairos is the specific, God-given moment for witness. Paul exhorts the Colossians to seize each opportunity for wise engagement with outsiders, combining gracious speech with the sharpness of salt (v. 6).

refs.note: The parallel master instruction uses identical logic: masters share a Master in heaven who shows no favouritism.
The exhortation to ‘make the most of every opportunity’ appears in both Ephesians and Colossians, confirming the twin letters’ common vocabulary.

notes.note: The master instruction completes the household code. The master’s authority is not absolute but accountable: he has a Master in heaven. MacArthur notes the radical levelling: the difference between slave and master is temporary and earthly; both answer to the same Lord.
Paul’s prayer request reveals his dependence on the church’s intercession, even from prison. The ‘open door’ metaphor is missional: Paul asks for opportunities to proclaim the mystery of Christ.
Wise conduct toward outsiders is the community’s daily evangelism. Speech ‘seasoned with salt’ is engaging, preservative, and distinctive—the verbal counterpart to the virtuous life of 3:12–17.

Calvin reads the exhortation as a call to winsome witness: the community’s conduct and speech are its primary evangelistic tools. Gracious speech does not mean flattery but truth delivered with courtesy and wisdom, adapted to the hearer’s needs.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase en chariti (with grace, graciously) could mean ‘attractive, winsome’ or ‘full of divine grace.’ The addition halati ērtymenon (seasoned with salt) is a culinary metaphor: speech should be flavourful, preservative, and stimulating.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Colossians and Philemon, Pillar (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo reads vv. 2–6 as the missional conclusion to the ethical section: the community’s internal life (ch. 3) must overflow into wise, gracious engagement with the outside world. Prayer, conduct, and speech are the three dimensions of the church’s missional posture.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Colossians, Philemon, WBC (1982) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien notes that the transition from household code to missional exhortation is seamless: the same community that orders its internal relationships under Christ’s lordship must also engage the surrounding culture with wisdom and grace.

hist.context: The household code concludes with a one-verse instruction to masters (v. 1): ‘provide your slaves with what is right and fair, because you know that you also have a Master in heaven.’ Paul then turns to prayer and mission (vv. 2–4): the community is to pray persistently, including for Paul’s own evangelistic work (‘that God may open a door for our message,’ v. 3). The exhortation to wise conduct toward outsiders (vv. 5–6) and gracious, seasoned speech completes the ethical section, emphasising the missional dimension of community life.

heb.paragraph: Paul’s closing greetings identify a network of co-workers (synergoi, vv. 10–11): Aristarchus, Mark, Jesus Justus, Epaphras, Luke, and Demas. The term synergos implies shared labour in the gospel mission, not merely personal friendship. This network sustains Paul’s ministry from prison and connects the scattered churches.

refs.note: The greeting list in Philemon overlaps significantly with Col 4:10–14: Epaphras, Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke appear in both, confirming the letters’ simultaneity.
The commendation of Tychicus in Ephesians uses virtually identical language to Col 4:7–8, confirming that both letters were carried by the same messenger.
The later reference to Demas’s desertion and Mark’s faithfulness provides the biographical sequel to Col 4:10, 14.

notes.note: Tychicus is the trusted letter-carrier; Onesimus is sent along with him—the same Onesimus of Philemon’s letter. MacArthur notes the pastoral sensitivity: by sending Onesimus with the authoritative carrier, Paul provides both protection and endorsement.

The greeting list reveals the diversity of Paul’s team: three Jewish Christians (Aristarchus, Mark, Justus—‘the only Jews among my co-workers for the kingdom of God,’ v. 11) and three Gentile Christians (Epaphras, Luke, Demas). MacArthur notes the poignancy of Demas’s inclusion: at this point he is faithful, but 2 Tim 4:10 reports his desertion.

The instructions to exchange letters with Laodicea reveal the practice of circular reading. The letter to Laodicea (v. 16) is now lost. The reminder to Archippus to ‘complete the ministry’ suggests a specific commission whose nature is unknown. Paul’s closing—‘remember my chains’—is both a plea for intercession and a reminder of the cost of the gospel.

Calvin reads the greetings as evidence of the communal nature of Paul’s ministry: the apostle is not a solitary genius but the leader of a team. Calvin notes the pastoral detail: Paul commends Mark to the Colossians, indicating prior instructions about receiving him. The autograph concluding sentence authenticates the entire letter and reminds the community of the apostle’s suffering.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase ho syn synaikchmalōtos mou (my fellow prisoner) applied to Aristarchus may be literal (imprisoned with Paul) or metaphorical (‘prisoner of war’ in the gospel’s service). The NET favours the literal reading.

The reference to the ‘letter from Laodicea’ (tēn ek Laodikias) has generated speculation: it may be a lost Pauline letter, a copy of Ephesians circulated under a different address, or the canonical Philemon. The NET acknowledges the uncertainty.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Colossians and Philemon, Pillar (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo reads the greetings as the relational infrastructure of the Pauline mission. The overlap with Philemon’s greeting list confirms that both letters were sent simultaneously and that the Onesimus situation is directly relevant to the Colossian community. The exchange of letters (v. 16) reveals the early church’s practice of treating apostolic correspondence as authoritative for multiple communities.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Colossians, Philemon, WBC (1982) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien notes that the greetings section, while seemingly routine, performs important theological and pastoral functions: it connects the isolated communities into a network, endorses specific individuals, and models the koinōnia that the letter’s theology demands. The autograph (v. 18) functions as a seal of apostolic authority.

hist.context: The greetings section (vv. 7–18) is one of the most informative in the Pauline corpus. Tychicus and Onesimus carry the letter (vv. 7–9)—connecting Colossians to Philemon. Six companions send greetings (vv. 10–14): Aristarchus (fellow prisoner), Mark (Barnabas’s cousin, now reconciled with Paul), Jesus called Justus (one of three Jewish co-workers), Epaphras (the Colossians’ own pastor, wrestling in prayer for them), Luke (the beloved physician), and Demas (who will later desert Paul, 2 Tim 4:10). Special instructions follow: greet the church in Laodicea, share letters between Colossae and Laodicea, and tell Archippus to complete his ministry (vv. 15–17). Paul’s autograph (‘I, Paul, write this greeting in my own hand,’ v. 18) authenticates the letter and closes with a request to remember his chains and a benediction of grace.

themes.note: Mission dominates through the prayer for open doors, wise conduct toward outsiders, and the missional network revealed in the greetings. Justice appears through the master’s accountability. Sovereignty undergirds the master-servant relationship.

rows.label: v.1
rows.text: Masters: provide what is right; you have a Master in heaven

rows.label: vv.2–4
rows.text: Prayer: persistence, open doors, proclaiming the mystery

rows.label: vv.5–6
rows.text: Wise conduct toward outsiders; speech seasoned with salt

rows.label: vv.10–14
rows.text: Greetings: Aristarchus, Mark, Justus, Epaphras, Luke, Demas

rows.label: vv.15–18
rows.text: Exchange letters with Laodicea; Archippus; Paul’s autograph

lit.note: Colossians 4 completes the household code (v. 1), provides missional instruction (vv. 2–6), and closes with an extended greeting section (vv. 7–18) that connects the Colossian community to the wider Pauline network and the simultaneous letter to Philemon.

hebtext.note: The verb exagorazomenoi (v. 5, ‘making the most of every opportunity’) literally means ‘buying up the time.’ The kairos is the specific, God-given moment for witness. Paul exhorts the Colossians to seize each opportunity for wise engagement with outsiders, combining gracious speech with the sharpness of salt (v. 6).

Paul’s closing greetings identify a network of co-workers (synergoi, vv. 10–11): Aristarchus, Mark, Jesus Justus, Epaphras, Luke, and Demas. The term synergos implies shared labour in the gospel mission, not merely personal friendship. This network sustains Paul’s ministry from prison and connects the scattered churches.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Luke
ppl.role: Physician; author of Luke-Acts; Paul’s companion
ppl.text: Luke the physician (Col 4:14) is identified by tradition as the author of Luke-Acts and as Paul’s companion in the "we" passages of Acts (16:10–17; 20:5–21:18; 27:1–28:16). He is with Paul during the second journey (Philippi onward), the voyage to Rome, and Paul’s imprisonment. Colossians 4:14 identifies him as "the beloved physician"; Philemon 24 calls him Paul’s fellow worker.

thread.text: The parallel master instruction uses identical logic: masters share a Master in heaven who shows no favouritism.
The exhortation to ‘make the most of every opportunity’ appears in both Ephesians and Colossians, confirming the twin letters’ common vocabulary.
The greeting list in Philemon overlaps significantly with Col 4:10–14: Epaphras, Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke appear in both, confirming the letters’ simultaneity.
The commendation of Tychicus in Ephesians uses virtually identical language to Col 4:7–8, confirming that both letters were carried by the same messenger.
The later reference to Demas’s desertion and Mark’s faithfulness provides the biographical sequel to Col 4:10, 14.

discourse.thesis: Continue in prayer; walk wisely toward outsiders — final greetings and instructions.

nodes.text: Continue steadfastly in prayer, being watchful. Pray that God may open a door for the word. Walk in wisdom toward outsiders. Let your speech be gracious, seasoned with salt.
Tychicus will tell them everything — a beloved brother and faithful minister. Paul sends him for this purpose, along with Onesimus, the faithful brother from Colossae.

Greetings from Aristarchus, Mark (receive him if he comes), Jesus Justus — the only Jews among Paul's co-workers. Epaphras greets them, always wrestling in prayers for their maturity. Luke the beloved physician greets them, and Demas.

Greet the brothers at Laodicea and Nympha's house church. Have this letter read in Laodicea; read the Laodicean letter also. Tell Archippus: fulfill the ministry you received. Paul's greeting by his own hand — remember his chains. Grace be with you.

coaching.tip: 'Devote yourselves to prayer, being watchful and thankful' (v. 2). Prayer is devotion, vigilance, and gratitude combined. 'Be wise in the way you act toward outsiders' (v. 5)—evangelism isn't argumentation but lifestyle. 'Conversation always full of grace, seasoned with salt' (v. 6). Salty speech preserves and flavors.

Greetings from Onesimus, Aristarchus, Mark, Epaphras, Luke, Demas (vv. 7-14). Behind doctrine stand people—co-workers, converts, prayer warriors. Demas, commended here, later deserts Paul (2 Tim 4:10). Even faithful communities contain future failures. Final greetings remind us: theology is lived in community.
